SpaceX & Starlink 
 
Electronics: $STM $FLTCF $TEL $APH $TXN 
 
If SpaceX is deploying an $85B capital raise, the biggest opportunity may not be SpaceX itself—but the entire supply chain that benefits from the spending. 
 
STM and TXN provide critical semiconductors and analog chips. TEL and APH are leaders in connectors and electronic components, deeply embedded in aerospace, communications, and data center infrastructure. FLTCF could benefit from rising demand for high-reliability electronics. 
 
Every satellite, every Starlink terminal, and every rocket launch requires advanced electronics and mission-critical components. Historically, some of the biggest winners are the companies selling the “picks and shovels,” not necessarily the gold miners.

four companies supplying the backbone of the semiconductor industry are setting up to break multi-year bases.  
 
$ENTG $ALGM $APH $DIOD  
 
This semiconductor “infrastructure stack” reflects a very clear market theme: as AI and advanced manufacturing scale, upstream materials and equipment providers become key beneficiaries. 
 
ENTG (Entegris) supplies advanced materials and contamination control systems critical for wafer yield. ALGM (Allegro MicroSystems) focuses on power and magnetic sensors, benefiting from EVs and industrial automation. APH (Amphenol) is a leader in high-speed connectors, directly tied to data center and AI server expansion. DIOD (Diodes Inc.) operates across analog and power semiconductors, serving industrial and automotive markets. 
 
From a supply-chain perspective, these companies do not “build AI” directly, but they form the hidden backbone of semiconductor scaling. As AI expands, demands for material purity, signal integrity, and power efficiency increase significantly. 
 
The market is gradually broadening from pure AI chip leaders into the full semiconductor ecosystem, where these names often outperform in mid-to-late cycle expansions. 
 
Is this the start of a long-term re-rating cycle, or just a cyclical extension within semiconductors? Do you prefer APH as a connectivity leader or ENTG as a “yield bottleneck” enabler?

Four names quietly sitting at the foundation of the semiconductor stack are starting to tighten up after long multi-year bases. 
 
$ENTG (materials & filtration) 
$ALGM (power & sensing) 
 $APH (interconnects) 
$DIOD (analog & discretes) are all showing early signs of structural basing rather than trend exhaustion. 
 
What stands out here isn’t just price action-it’s duration. These are not short consolidations; they are extended bases that often precede meaningful re-rating when cycle momentum returns. 
 
In semis, leadership usually starts at the top (AI compute), but the second wave is where the “plumbing” names catch up-materials, power, interconnects, and analog layers that enable the entire stack. 
 
If the broader semiconductor cycle continues to grind higher, these are exactly the kinds of under-owned infrastructure names that tend to break out later, not earlier. 
 
Long base, higher potential expansion.
$APH $CLS $SMCI $VRT

Semianalysis has identified deepening bottlenecks in server rack systems 
 
While the industry spent years racing to solve macro-level power grid constraints, the binding constraint has moving inward—collapsing into highly concentrated architectural bottlenecks at the individual server rack level 
 
As next-gen AI clusters scale to 100,000+ GPUs, packing massive compute density into single physical frames has triggered severe physical & engineering limitations 
 
Because the rack has evolved from a simple metal enclosure into a deeply complex, liquid-cooled, high-voltage electrical instrument, the role of server assemblers has fundamentally shifted 
 
ODMs (such as $CLS $SMCI  & Foxconn) are no longer just building parts; they are critical engineering integration partners. Component vendors specializing in these rack-level bottlenecks—such as $APH  (high-speed cabling) & $VRT  (thermal and power management)—have become the gatekeepers of the AI buildout.

Amphenol shares initially fell 6.6% after Nvidia unveiled its new Rubin AI chip platform at CES 2026, which features a cable-free design, sparking investor concern for suppliers of connectors. However, the stock rebounded and traded up 0.7% to $140.84 by the close. 
Analysts at Evercore ISI noted that while Rubin eliminates some cabling, it will require denser use of connectors to handle higher speed, power, and integration demands. Amphenol, a key provider of cables, sensors, and connectors for Nvidia’s data centers, is expected to see its content share rise 20%–40% compared with the previous Blackwell platform. Evercore highlighted that Amphenol remains a critical enabler of next-generation AI infrastructure, benefiting from the ongoing AI hardware expansion. The firm maintained its Outperform rating and $150 price target on the stock.
